title: FastAPI
definition: A modern, high-performance Python web framework for building REST APIs based on Python type hints and Pydantic. Automatically generates OpenAPI documentation and validates requests/responses.
why_used: Fastest Python web framework for building ML inference APIs. Auto-generates Swagger docs, enforces type safety via Pydantic, and handles async requests efficiently.
interview_point: FastAPI uses Python type hints for automatic request validation and documentation. It's built on Starlette (ASGI) and supports async/await natively — allowing concurrent inference requests without blocking. Much faster than Flask for high-concurrency ML serving.
advantages: Very fast (~300% faster than Flask in benchmarks)
real_world_example:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
import numpy as np
import pickle

app = FastAPI(title='ML Inference API', version='1.0')

# Load model at startup
with open('model.pkl', 'rb') as f:
    model = pickle.load(f)

class PredictRequest(BaseModel):
    features: list[float]
    model_name: str = 'default'

class PredictResponse(BaseModel):
    prediction: float
    confidence: float

@app.post('/predict', response_model=PredictResponse)
async def predict(request: PredictRequest):
    X = np.array(request.features).reshape(1, -1)
    pred = model.predict(X)[0]
    proba = model.predict_proba(X)[0].max()
    return PredictResponse(prediction=float(pred), confidence=float(proba))

@app.get('/health')
async def health():
    return {'status': 'healthy'}

# Run: uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --workers 4
```

title: Streamlit
definition: A Python library for rapidly building interactive web applications for machine learning and data science without frontend knowledge. Any Python script becomes a shareable web app.
why_used: Fastest way to build ML demos and internal data tools. No HTML/CSS/JavaScript required — pure Python. Ideal for showcasing ML models to stakeholders.
interview_point: Streamlit reruns the entire script top to bottom on any user interaction. st.cache_data and st.cache_resource prevent expensive recomputation. Session state (st.session_state) persists values across reruns.
advantages: Built-in widgets for sliders, file upload, charts
limitations: Reruns entire script on every interaction
real_world_example:
```python
import streamlit as st
import pandas as pd
import pickle
import plotly.express as px

st.set_page_config(page_title='ML Demo', page_icon='🤖')
st.title('Machine Learning Model Demo')

@st.cache_resource  # Cache model loading
def load_model():
    with open('model.pkl', 'rb') as f:
        return pickle.load(f)

model = load_model()

# Sidebar inputs
with st.sidebar:
    st.header('Input Features')
    age = st.slider('Age', 18, 80, 35)
    income = st.number_input('Income', 0, 1000000, 50000)

# Predict
if st.button('Predict', type='primary'):
    X = [[age, income]]
    pred = model.predict(X)[0]
    proba = model.predict_proba(X)[0]
    st.success(f'Prediction: {pred}')
    st.metric('Confidence', f'{proba.max():.1%}')
```

title: Docker
definition: A platform for packaging applications and their dependencies into isolated, lightweight containers. Ensures the ML model runs identically in development, staging, and production regardless of the host environment.
why_used: Eliminates 'works on my machine' problems. Containers package the ML model, Python version, libraries, and configuration together — making deployment reproducible and scalable.
interview_point: Docker vs Virtual Machines: Docker containers share the host OS kernel (much lighter, faster startup, ~MBs). VMs include full OS (~GBs, minutes to start). Multi-stage builds reduce image size by separating build dependencies from runtime — critical for lean ML serving containers.
advantages: Easy scaling and orchestration with Kubernetes
real_world_example:
```dockerfile
# Multi-stage build for lean ML serving image
FROM python:3.11-slim AS builder

WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

FROM python:3.11-slim AS runner
WORKDIR /app

# Copy only installed packages from builder
COPY --from=builder /usr/local/lib/python3.11 /usr/local/lib/python3.11
COPY --from=builder /usr/local/bin /usr/local/bin

COPY . .

EXPOSE 8000

# Healthcheck
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s \
  CMD curl -f http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1

CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000", "--workers", "4"]
```

title: Gradio
definition: A Python library for building ML model demos with minimal code. Provides pre-built interfaces for text, image, audio, video inputs/outputs and integrates directly with Hugging Face Spaces for free hosting.
why_used: Even faster than Streamlit for simple model demos. Built-in support for multimodal inputs (images, audio, video). Native Hugging Face integration for easy sharing.
interview_point: Gradio Blocks gives full layout control; gr.Interface is the quick-start approach. Gradio automatically handles batching and queuing for concurrent requests. Hugging Face Spaces hosts Gradio apps for free — just push to a repo.
advantages: Fastest way to demo multimodal models
Free hosting on Hugging Face Spaces
limitations: Less flexible than Streamlit for complex apps
real_world_example:
```python
import gradio as gr
from transformers import pipeline

# Load model
classifier = pipeline('sentiment-analysis')

def analyze_sentiment(text):
    result = classifier(text)[0]
    return f"{result['label']}: {result['score']:.3f}"

# Simple Interface
demo = gr.Interface(
    fn=analyze_sentiment,
    inputs=gr.Textbox(placeholder='Enter text to analyze...'),
    outputs=gr.Textbox(label='Sentiment'),
    title='Sentiment Analysis',
    description='Analyze the sentiment of any text'
)

demo.launch(share=True)  # share=True generates a public URL
```

title: Inference Latency
definition: The time it takes for a machine learning model to make a prediction on a new input after receiving the request. Usually measured in milliseconds (ms).
why_used: Critical for real-time user experiences (e.g., search, recommendations, autonomous driving) where slow responses lead to user drop-off or system failure.
interview_point: Latency vs Throughput: Latency is the time for a single query to complete; throughput is queries processed per second (QPS). Optimizations for latency include: model quantization, ONNX runtime conversion, GPU batching, and feature caching (using Redis).
advantages: Reduces server cost under load when optimized
limitations: Lowering latency often requires compromising accuracy (e.g., quantization, using smaller models)
real_world_example:
```python
import time

start_time = time.perf_counter()
prediction = model.predict(input_data)
latency_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start_time) * 1000
print(f'Inference Latency: {latency_ms:.2f} ms')
```

title: Processing Pipeline Time
definition: The duration required to process a data payload completely through all stages of an engineering pipeline, from input ingestion to final formatted output.
why_used: Helps locate bottlenecks in multi-stage systems (e.g. data decoding, feature extraction, model inference, and output formatting) to ensure SLAs are met.
interview_point: When debugging pipeline time, profile each stage individually. Typical culprits include unvectorized preprocessing, synchronous network I/O in loops, or inefficient serialization (like using pickle for huge payloads vs. Protobuf or Arrow).
limitations: Can vary depending on machine hardware load and resource sharing
real_world_example:
```python
import time

def run_pipeline(data):
    t0 = time.time()
    x1 = load_step(data)
    x2 = process_step(x1)
    x3 = render_step(x2)
    duration = time.time() - t0
    print(f'Pipeline completed in {duration:.3f} seconds')
    return x3
```

title: API Response Latency
definition: The total time elapsed from when a client sends an HTTP/gRPC request to when it receives the complete response from the API server. Includes network transit time and server processing time.
why_used: Core KPI for measuring network service quality and user-perceived responsiveness of web-based APIs.
interview_point: API response latency is affected by network round-trip time (RTT), DNS resolution, TLS handshake overhead, and server execution time. To minimize it, use HTTP/2 or HTTP/3, implement global CDNs, keep connections alive, and optimize database/inference queries.
advantages: Directly maps to user experience quality
Easily measurable from the client side
limitations: Heavily influenced by client network quality and geographical distance from the server
real_world_example:
```python
import requests
import time

start = time.perf_counter()
response = requests.get('https://api.yourservice.com/health')
response_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
print(f'API Response Latency: {response_time:.2f} ms')
# Or using requests built-in elapsed duration:
print(f'Server elapsed time: {response.elapsed.total_seconds() * 1000:.2f} ms')
```

title: Real-Time Inference
definition: The process of generating model predictions on-demand as new input data arrives, requiring low latency (usually under 100ms) to support interactive user interfaces.
why_used: Necessary for live applications like chatbot responses, real-time medical screening, fraud detection, and autocomplete utilities.
interview_point: Real-Time (Online) vs Batch Inference: Online inference processes requests individually as they arrive, optimized for low latency. Batch inference runs periodically on large volumes of data, optimized for high throughput. Real-time serving requires load balancing, auto-scaling, and often model optimization (e.g. TensorRT, ONNX).
limitations: Must handle spikes in traffic concurrency
